title: There Is a Mountain Lion in the Datacenter
description: # There Is a Mountain Lion in the Datacenter *P-22 in the backyard, EINTR in the syscall return, and the oldest guild fight in computing.* ![A mountain lion walks down the aisle of a hyperscale datacenter, rack LEDs reflecting off the fur.](https://d18pmf4kv0skwk.cloudfront.
fullText:
P-22 in the backyard, EINTR in the syscall return, and the oldest guild fight in computing.

On the twelfth of December, 2022, a Los Feliz homeowner walked out the back door of a house in the hills above Hollywood and found a mountain lion sleeping under the deck. The homeowner called the California Department of Fish and Wildlife. The lion, tagged P-22, had been the unofficial mascot of Los Angeles for a decade. He had crossed the 405 and the 101 to reach Griffith Park, lived in the middle of a city of four million people, been blamed for the death of a koala at the LA Zoo, been photographed in front of the Hollywood sign, and ended up under a Los Feliz deck because he had been hit by a car and was dying. He was euthanized five days later. The city wrote love letters in the LA Times.

The homeowner who found him was not in any moral sense being invaded. The lion had the older claim. Griffith Park existed before the suburb did. The hills had been a lion habitat for as long as there had been hills. The Los Feliz house was the new arrival, built on the assumption that the design of the house and the city around it would keep the wilderness at a polite distance. The design turned out to be insufficient on the morning of December 12th. The lion had an opinion about decks.

That is the relationship between the systems programmer and the application programmer in modern infrastructure. The application programmer built the suburb. The systems programmer is the lion. The wilderness, in this telling, is the hardware: the cache, the page table, the syscall return value, the signal-delivery race, the CCX boundary, the four kinds of memory the engineer now has to keep straight. The application programmer was sold a model of the machine in which the wilderness had been kept at a polite distance. The wilderness has a different view of the matter, and increasingly the view shows up under the deck at three in the morning.

A couple of weeks ago I wrote about a YouTube video that compressed forty years of C++ history into one geographic slogan and treated the existence of POSIX EINTR as evidence that the operating system was "freaking out." The piece focused on the rhetorical compression. What it did not name, because the piece was already long enough, is the prejudice underneath the rhetorical move. The complaint that read() returns EINTR to userspace, when stripped of the dramatic terminal demo, is the complaint of a Los Feliz homeowner finding a mountain lion under their deck. The complaint is that the wilderness was supposed to stay in the wilderness. The reply is that it never did, and the suburb was built on the assumption that it would.

It is the oldest guild fight in computing. It is older than the video, older than C++, older than Unix in any form that the modern reader would recognize. It is the fight between the people who believe the operating system exists to launder hardware reality away from the application programmer, and the people who believe the application programmer has to know enough hardware reality to ship a system that runs.

The cleanest way to name the divide is not by salary, not by geography, not by ethnicity, not by the colour of anyone's badge. It is by the layer of the stack where the engineer signs their commits.

The application programmer writes code above the syscall boundary. Their world is composed of types, control flow, and abstractions the language gives them. Their mental model of the machine is the model the language presents. If the language hides a thing, that thing does not exist in their workspace. When the abstraction leaks, it is a bug in the abstraction, not a feature of the world.

The systems programmer writes code that touches the boundary. Their world includes the cache hierarchy, the page table, the syscall table, the NIC's descriptor ring, the scheduler's runqueue, and the signal-delivery race the kernel can produce between any two instructions. Their mental model of the machine is the machine. When the abstraction leaks, it is the abstraction confessing what was always there.

The two populations do not necessarily dislike each other. They have, however, been talking past each other for fifty years, and a recurring feature of the conversation is the application programmer treating the systems programmer's concerns as evidence of the systems programmer's bad taste. The systems programmer's reply is usually a measurement.

Edsger Dijkstra wrote On the cruelty of really teaching computing science in December 1988 (EWD 1036). The essay is the cleanest single statement in the literature of the position that computing should be taught as a branch of mathematics, that the engineering side of the discipline is at best vulgar, and that industry is too stupid to recognize the gift the academy is trying to hand it. The prejudice is so completely undisguised that no paraphrase improves on it. Dijkstra opens the indictment of the field most adjacent to his own:

Software engineering, of course, presents itself as another worthy cause, but that is eyewash: if you carefully read its literature and analyse what its devotees actually do, you will discover that software engineering has accepted as its charter "How to program if you cannot."

He labels it "The Doomed Discipline," doomed because its goal is "self-contradictory." Computer engineering is dismissed in a single dependent clause for wanting "to continue to act as if it is all only a matter of higher bit rates and more flops per second." The business community is "mentally unprepared to accept that they only solve the easier problems at the price of creating much harder ones." The educational establishment is on track for "each next stage of infantilization of the curriculum" being "hailed as educational progress."

The proposal at the end of the essay is the prejudice in its purest form. Dijkstra describes a freshman programming course built entirely around the predicate calculus, and adds:

In order to drive home the message that this introductory programming course is primarily a course in formal mathematics, we see to it that the programming language in question has not been implemented on campus so that students are protected from the temptation to test their programs.

A computer science department, in this telling, should deny its students a working compiler on principle. That is the position. That is what he meant by it.

Dijkstra returned to the same well twelve years later in EWD 1305, an "Answers to questions from students of Software Engineering" piece dated November 2000, in which he reprises a line of his own from 1985 (EWD 920): "If the computer industry can be saved, only computing science can do it." This is the manifesto in one sentence. The discipline of writing code that runs is, in this telling, an emergency. The discipline of proving programs correct on paper is, in this telling, the cure.

You can let the snobs indict themselves. Few do it as completely as Dijkstra.

Richard Gabriel's Worse is Better, the essay the YouTube video misreads, is the systems-programmer reply, written from inside the AI/Lisp community that Dijkstra would have recognized as one of his constituencies. Gabriel's central technical example is the read() syscall and the so-called PC loser-ing problem: the question of whether, when a system call is interrupted by a signal, the kernel should restart the call transparently or return EINTR and dump the problem on the user. Gabriel called the MIT position "the right thing" and called the New Jersey position "worse is better," then watched the latter win for forty years.

He kept arguing with himself about it. A few months after the original essay, he wrote Worse Is Better Is Worse under a pseudonym. Then he wrote Is Worse Really Better? and refused to pick a side. Then he wrote one more in 2000 leaning back toward "the right thing." The full set is on his site. That a man could spend a decade rebutting himself in public is itself evidence that the question is harder than the YouTube headline. It is also evidence that the application-programmer side of the argument, the side that wants read() to never return EINTR, was being defended by the strongest thinker in the room and still failed to displace the practitioner consensus.

The practitioner consensus, then and now, is that the three-line EINTR loop is fine. It is documented, it is small, every Unix programmer has typed it a thousand times. The argument that the operating system is "freaking out" because it surfaced the signal to userspace is an argument about taste, not about cost. The cost of the retry loop is three lines. The cost of hiding the signal from userspace is that the application can no longer choose what to do about it.

The cleanest version of this fight I ever had was not about read(). It was about send().

For years, the chief complaint from the application-programmer audience for an HFT library I maintained was that the TCP object's send() method returned a value instead of being declared void. The users wanted the platform to handle it. The order goes out. The order arrives. End of story. They wanted to write tcp.send(order) on a line by itself and move on to the next line as if the syscall had completed and the bytes were on the wire. What they wanted was for the kernel's socket buffer to be invisible.

The kernel's socket buffer is not invisible. It is a fixed-size queue that fills up under load, exactly the kind of load an HFT desk produces when a price moves. When send() returns short, or returns EAGAIN on a non-blocking socket, the platform has just told the caller something material: your previous order is still in the outbox; I have not transmitted it yet; the exchange has not seen it. If the caller ignores that signal and chains a second order behind the first, the second order is going into a market the caller is no longer correctly modelling. The price the caller computed against was the price a hundred microseconds ago. The timestamp the order will carry is the timestamp it eventually gets sent at, which is later than the caller thinks, sometimes by enough to violate the firm's own compliance rules about order-event ordering. The desk does not lose money because the platform did the wrong thing. The desk loses money because the platform told the truth and the application code refused to listen.

The fix, exactly as in the EINTR case, was three lines. Check the return value. If the buffer is full, either retry, or scale back the strategy, or signal the upstream pricer that the market is moving faster than we can talk to it and to prepare for a reversal. With C++20 coroutines the fix is even shorter, because co_await on the socket hides the loop without hiding the decision. The decision is the part that mattered. The decision is what the application programmer kept asking the platform to make on their behalf, and what the platform could not make on their behalf because the platform did not know what the strategy wanted to do when the wire was congested.

The reason this case is worth telling is that the request was not for correctness. The request was for aesthetic relief. The application programmers wanted their hot-path code to look clean. They wanted send on a line, on its own, with no return-value handling, no branch, no loop. They wanted the file to read like the slide. The cost of that aesthetic was paid downstream, in the production incidents that followed any market dislocation big enough to fill the kernel socket buffer for more than a few milliseconds. Desks blew up. The post-mortems consistently identified the ignored return value. The next library version still got the same complaint about send() not being void.

At some point, out of despair, I tagged send() with [[nodiscard]], the C++17 attribute that asks the compiler to warn when a non-void return value is thrown away. C++20 lets you supply a reason string, so the warning could literally read "send() may return short on a congested socket; check the value before issuing the next order." The compiler now told the application programmer, at build time, that the bug they kept shipping was a bug. The response was not "thank you for catching this." The response was phone calls about the warnings. Desk builds were now noisy. Could I please turn it off. Could I please make the attribute conditional. Could I please ship a send_unchecked() variant so the existing call sites would compile cleanly again. The bug was not the bug; the warning about the bug was the bug. The compiler was, in the Lori-Wired sense, freaking out, and freaking out was what the desk wanted it to stop doing.

The aesthetic complaint, it turned out, was a cover. The real reason the warnings were intolerable was that they removed plausible deniability. Before [[nodiscard]], when a desk blew up on a congested send, the post-mortem could be steered toward the platform team: the library never told us this could happen, the documentation was buried, we were following the example in the header. After [[nodiscard]], every clean build was a written record that the compiler had warned the developer, in the developer's own translation unit, on the developer's own machine, the morning before the incident. Compliance would now fall on the desk, not on the core team. The attribute had moved the blame surface by exactly one floor. The phone calls were not about taste. They were about who was going to be standing in the room when the regulator asked who knew what, and when.

The same fight, in the same shape Gabriel described in 1989, with the same losing side, on a trading floor in the 2020s. The only thing that had changed in thirty-five years was that the compiler had been given a way to take the systems programmer's side, and the application programmer had learned to ask for the compiler to be silenced instead.

The application-programmer position has been tried, in production, multiple times, by serious people, and the results are in. The list is not short.

GNU Hurd is the canonical entry. Started in 1990, designed as a microkernel architecture on Mach with translators in userspace, intended to be the GNU project's official kernel. Thirty-five years later it is still not the GNU project's official kernel. Linux, the unprincipled monolith written by a Finnish student over a summer, is. The technical purity of the Hurd architecture was not in dispute. The shipping kernel was Linux.

The Tanenbaum-Torvalds debate in 1992 (archived as Appendix A of Open Sources) is the same fight in real time, with both sides represented by serious operating systems people. Tanenbaum called Linux "a giant step back into the 1970s." Torvalds conceded that, "from a theoretical (and aesthetical) standpoint linux looses" [sic], and shipped Linux anyway. Three decades later the theoretical and aesthetical superiority of the microkernel is still being asserted, and Linux is what runs the data centres, the phones, the cars, the satellites, and the trading floors.

Lisp machines lost a war that, in the application-programmer telling, they should have won. Beautiful environment, image-based development, the language designed by people who thought about language design for a living. They lost because stock workstations got faster, the AI winter cut funding, and Common Lisp standardization arrived after the platform was already dead. The systems-programmer reply to "why did Lisp lose" is that the runtime cost model did not match what the customer was willing to pay.

The pattern is consistent. The beautiful system gets the paper. The ugly system gets the deployment. Rob Pike noticed this and wrote Systems Software Research Is Irrelevant about it in 2000, from inside Bell Labs, with the bitterness of a man who had spent twenty years watching research labs build beautiful systems that nobody used and industry build ugly ones that ran the world.

For most of the history of the divide, the application programmer could pretend the hardware was uniform. The Intel ring bus and the big shared L3 cache made it look as if a CPU was a single thing you could talk to without thinking about which core you were on. The abstraction was leaky even then (NUMA had existed since the 1990s, false sharing was a real cost since the late nineties) but the leak was small enough that you could ignore it if you wrote application code and let somebody else worry about the rest.

AMD's Zen architecture in 2017, and especially the multi-CCX EPYC and Threadripper parts that followed, made the leak structural. The L3 cache on a Zen part is not a single shared resource; it is sliced per-CCX, and crossing the CCX boundary costs you cache-coherence latency you can measure with a stopwatch. A producer-consumer queue that lives entirely within one CCX runs at one speed. The same queue across CCXs runs at another. The application programmer who does not know which cores share which slice of L3 will ship the slow version by default, because the language does not know either.

This is not a niche concern. The Zen geometry is what every cloud provider, every HFT firm, every database team, every game-engine team had to learn the hard way between 2017 and 2020. There is no library that hides it. There is no compiler flag that makes the topology go away. The systems programmer's claim, that the application programmer needs to know what their hardware looks like, became impossible to refuse once the market leader started shipping parts where the cost of not knowing was a measurable factor in your tail latency.

The same lesson is written into every modern stack. SIMD is the most recent battleground: I wrote a piece on std::simd arguing that a library cannot fix a language problem, and the broader version of that argument is that a language cannot fix a hardware problem either. If the hardware has lanes, the engineer needs lanes. If the cache is sliced, the engineer needs to know about the slice. If the syscall can be interrupted, the engineer needs to know that, too.

The argument so far has been general. Here is the specific case. In the textbook, a network stack has seven layers (physical, data link, network, transport, session, presentation, application), each implemented by a different subsystem, each cleanly separated from the next by an interface the layer above is forbidden to peek beneath. For most of the industry, that picture survives more or less intact. For HFT it has been progressively demolished since roughly 2008, layer by layer, by the systems engineers who decided the textbook was costing them money.

The first wall came down with kernel bypass. Solarflare's OpenOnload was presented publicly in February 2008. Myricom's DBL shipped in September 2010. Mellanox VMA arrived through the Voltaire acquisition in November 2010. Intel's DPDK was distributed to early customers the same month, with the BSD-licensed public release following in 2013. Each of these did the same thing in different packaging: take the kernel out of the hot path entirely. The application now talked to the NIC's descriptor rings directly. The socket API became a relic. Transport, session, and the application all started running in the same address space, on the same core, with no system call between them.

The second wall came down with FPGA SmartNICs. NovaSparks shipped a pure-FPGA feed handler starting in 2008 and a pure-FPGA order book builder in 2012. Algo-Logic launched in 2009, Enyx in 2011. Solarflare's ApplicationOnload Engine (SFA6902F) shipped in January 2013, putting an Altera Stratix V on the NIC card itself. Exablaze's ExaNIC X10 in February 2016 broke the microsecond barrier for TCP/UDP. The pattern was uniform: the feed handler, the risk checks, and the order generator all moved off the host CPU and into FPGA logic on the wire. Data link, network, transport, and a piece of application now lived together on the same silicon, with no boundary the textbook would recognize.

The third wall came down at the switch. Arista's 7124SX in March 2011 was the first sub-500 ns cut-through 10GbE switch. The 7124FX in March 2012 bolted an Altera FPGA into the switch chassis. Cisco's Nexus 3548 with Algo Boost shipped in September 2012 with 190 ns warp-mode forwarding. By 2013 Metamako had introduced the layer-1 cross-connect at 4 ns. Arista acquired Metamako in September 2018 and the 7130L Series in March 2019 productized the L1 + FPGA platform at deterministic 5 ns. The network layer, as a concept, has been compressed at this point to wire propagation plus a single SerDes round-trip. There is nothing left to optimize at that layer except where you stand in the data centre relative to the matching engine.

The fourth wall is the one currently coming down: ASIC. Jump Trading was hiring an ASIC team publicly by 2017. Hudson River Trading, Jane Street, and Optiver have followed. The piece on this site about the cut-through and the layered stack covers the Versal MRMAC and Alveo UL3524 sub-3 ns SerDes era in detail. The destination is clear enough: the matching logic, the risk gate, the wire, and the silicon merge into a single tape-out, and the textbook layering is no longer a useful description of anything.

Overlay the social picture on the technical one. The application programmer in HFT was hired, in many cases, on the strength of credentials that emphasised algorithms, data structures, language fluency, the things the CS curriculum teaches. They arrived expecting the seven-layer picture to hold, expecting the kernel to handle the syscalls, expecting the language to abstract the machine, expecting the abstractions to be sound. Then the systems engineers next to them spent fifteen years cutting holes in every layer they were standing on, until the floor was mostly holes and the application programmer was being asked to know about the holes.

The memory hierarchy is the cleanest example of what came through the holes. The application programmer was taught, in school, that memory is a flat array indexed by a pointer. The C abstract machine still believes this. The actual machine in front of the HFT engineer in 2026 has L1 cache, L2 cache, L3 cache sliced per CCX on Zen parts, DRAM that is NUMA-local or NUMA-remote depending on which socket the allocator handed you, hugepages that you have to request explicitly, HBM on the accelerator if you have one, CXL-attached memory if you are at the front of the curve, and the FPGA's on-chip BRAM and URAM if you have collapsed enough of the stack to be writing RTL. Each of these is a different memory, with a different latency, a different bandwidth, a different coherence guarantee, and a different cost of getting it wrong. The flat array exists only in the textbook.

The complaint "why am I being forced to understand that there are two, or three, or four kinds of memory" has the same shape as the complaint "why is read() returning EINTR." It is the Los Feliz homeowner asking somebody to please put the lion back in Griffith Park. The lion was always in Griffith Park. What changed is that the firm's tail-latency budget started being measured in tens of nanoseconds, and at that budget the wilderness is no longer optional reading. There is no version of this story where the park retreats. There is only the version where the suburb learns to read tracks.

Mike Acton's Data-Oriented Design keynote at CppCon 2014 is the contemporary classic. The line that the room remembers is "the purpose of all programs, and all parts of those programs, is to transform data from one form to another." The line that the room should remember is the one immediately around it: most programmers do not know what they are doing, the OOP-curriculum mindset is incompatible with hardware reality, and the way out is to stop building software as if the machine were the abstraction the textbook claimed it was.

Casey Muratori's "Clean" Code, Horrible Performance (2023) is the direct sequel. The argument is no longer abstract. Muratori writes the canonical Clean Code shape (virtual dispatch hierarchy, polymorphism, the textbook OO design) and measures it against successively more direct rewrites of the same logic. Replacing the polymorphism with a switch is already a 1.5x win. Replacing the switch with table-driven dispatch, then collapsing the loop, takes the cumulative gap past 10x. The systems-programmer position is no longer a matter of taste; it is a measurement.

Joel Spolsky was already making the academic version of the complaint in The Perils of JavaSchools in 2005. The CS departments had stopped teaching pointers, and graduates were arriving in industry unable to reason about memory. He framed it as a hiring problem. It was always also a curriculum problem.

These voices do not agree on remedies. Acton wants data-oriented design. Muratori wants the textbook off the shelf. Spolsky wants the curriculum changed. They agree on the diagnosis: the application-programmer worldview has been sold a model of the machine that the machine does not honour.

The previous piece on this site argued that the Stroustrup video had a chorus of agreement from the people who actually built C++: look the trivia up; the model in head is what matters. The model in head, for the engineer who has to ship at HFT cycle counts, is a model of the machine. It is the cache, the page, the queue, the signal, the syscall. It is the part of the world the application programmer's curriculum was supposed to make optional and could not, because no curriculum can override physics.

The complaint that POSIX returns EINTR to userspace is, in this telling, a complaint that the world is not the world the textbook described. The right answer is not to redesign the world. The right answer is to update the textbook.

We have been having this argument since 1988. Dijkstra has been dead for twenty-three years, and the "doomed discipline" he tried to indict has shipped every kernel that matters.

title: That an app 'Fits on a Floppy' is still a useful measure in 2026
description: In a world of mass-produced bot-slopware, small is more beautiful than ever
fullText:
In a world of mass-produced bot-slopware, small is more beautiful than ever

If you're old enough, you might remember using floppy disks, either of the 3.5-inch or 5.25-inch variety. They didn't hold much and often you had to have many disks to install one program. Don't be misled by Fits on a Floppy's retro-tech name: it is most
definitely not about 20th century data media. It's about compactness and
comprehensibility.

Fits on a Floppy describes
itself as "a Manifesto for Small Software," and as we read it, we found
ourselves nodding in agreement, right from the opening line:

That is certainly the impression of this author, and it is not just
us. We are irresistibly reminded of the Red Hat developer's six waves of
industry BS that we recounted
in February.

Like any eternal verity of the computing industry, there's even an XKCD comic about it, if you needed
any more persuading. XKCD's own internal citations, both about voting machines and indeed about the use of the blockchain,
reinforce the message. Randall Monroe spells it out:

That sounds about right. And parenthetically, anyone who says that
they can improve anything with either blockchain or AI
is no more to be trusted than a schoolteacher who gives no homework.

A year before that Red Hat engineer talked to us about waves of
industry BS, Belgian consultant Bert
Hubert talked to The Reg about digital sovereignty – and he
feels similarly. In 2024, he wrote A
2024 Plea for Lean Software in tribute to the great Niklaus Wirth,
who passed away earlier that month.

In our own obituary
for Professor Wirth, we mentioned the 1995 paper that inspired
Hubert: A Plea for Lean
Software (this is a PDF of a scan – but we have posted a more
readable plain
text version here).

One of the lasting effects of that paper is what is now called Wirth's Law:

Sadly, that seems to have been its main impact. As a usable
demonstration of his 2024 "plea," Hubert offered a working example, which
he explained in Trifecta
Technology. It's a web image-sharing tool, implemented in under
2000 lines of code. There's also a page for the Trifecta app
itself, which comes in as a 1.7 MB compressed Docker file. (With
some clever
disk formatting, you could get that
on a 1.4 MB floppy.)

There are, as that anonymous Red Hatter observed, so many different
layers of unnecessary complication and plain old marketing lies in
modern IT that it is now hard to even keep track of them. One point of
the Fits on a Floppy idea is that if you impose an artificial
limit on project size, merely by keeping it very small, you will be
forced to keep it very simple. That simplicity is the goal here, not
fitting on 1980s physical media.

You might react with scorn when you hear the idea that in the 2020s,
anything useful could fit into under 1.5 MB. When even a leading tool to
write an ISO file onto a USB key is a hundred times that size, it sounds absurd. But it really is not.

The mind behind the manifesto is developer Matt Sephton, and he
offers 18 tiny but useful apps that he's written to prove his point –
plus a screensaver which we feel sure is an hommage to
Berkeley's classic
Flying Toasters screensaver.

Others are still making useful single-floppy-sized apps today. We
wrote about the revival
of the Dillo web browser, and at last year's FOSDEM, the project
lead was handing out
floppies with the latest release. The whole app, on one diskette. Drew
DeVault's Hare programming language is still in
development, but when it reaches version 1.0, he plans to sell copies on a floppy:

Another tiny modern language is the Janet Language. It's not
quite so small, but its just over 2MB
download could fit onto the 2.8 MB floppies that were used
in later IBM PS/2 models and the NeXTstation.

The real point here is about the readability and long term
maintainability of compact, even minimalistic code. It's a similar point
to that made in Dave Gauer's Ascetic Computing
essay, which we cited and linked to when looking
at OpenBSD 7.9.

Small size and simplicity is what Fits on a Floppy is really
talking about, not about physical media. He explicitly spells it out for
the hard-of-thinking:

Bert Hubert too returned to this theme when he wrote a piece On
Long Term Software Development.

At this
year's Open Source Policy Summit, we saw some pundits pontificating
that to escape the US cloud, the answer was that Europe needed its
own companies running their own datacenters running
Europe's own domestic cloud.

This is so manifestly Getting The Wrong End Of The Stick that it put
us in mind of Wolfgang Pauli's famous line: "That is not only not right;
it is not even wrong."

The way to escape a broken model that was a bad
idea in the first place is not to make your own sovereign version. All
you're doing is locking yourself in your own personal cage. The smart
answer is to discard the broken model, and go back to an older, simpler
model where organizations own and store their own data on their own
servers.

As ever, the KISS
Principle is one of the best guidelines. It's Occam's Razor in
reverse: the best solution is the simplest possible solution. If the
problem is that you are trapped in someone else's cloud, then don't
switch to another cloud and risk it happening again: get your private
data out of the cloud altogether. Just Use One Big
Server. Hire some grumpy old techies with grey hair (or none) to run
it – there are plenty out there, but ageism keeps them out of work.

At the smallest and most local end of this scale, then one useful
guiding principle is to just keep the tools as small as you can possibly
make them. It's an artificial limit, but that does not lessen its
validity. It's not the only way. It may not even be the best way. But
it's a way, a simple, clear, obvious way – and there's nothing to
prevent anyone finding their own different path to radical
simplicity.

The PC rose to greatness running on two 360 kB floppy drives – hard
disk drives only came along later. Tools like Lotus 1-2-3 redefined
business management running on one 360 kB disk, with a second 360 kB
data disk in drive B: – and this vulture is willing to bet that some
spreadsheets built on such machines, long since converted to Microsoft
Excel, are still running multinationals, and indeed nations, today.
Compared to that, 1.4 MB is luxurious. ®

title: Nobel laureate invents machine that harvests water from dry air
description: Omar Yaghi’s invention uses ambient thermal energy and can generate up to 1,000 litres of clean water every day
fullText:
Omar Yaghi’s invention uses ambient thermal energy and can generate up to 1,000 litres of clean water every day

A Nobel laureate’s environmentally friendly invention that provides clean water if central supplies are knocked out by a hurricane or drought could be a life saver for vulnerable islands, its founder says.

The invention, by the chemist Prof Omar Yaghi, uses a type of science called reticular chemistry to create molecularly engineered materials, which can extract moisture from the air and harvest water even in arid and desert conditions.

Atoco, a technology company that Yaghi founded, said its units, comparable in size to a 20ft shipping container and powered entirely by ultra-low-grade thermal energy, could be placed in local communities to generate up to 1,000 litres of clean water every day, even if centralised electricity and water sources were interrupted by drought or storm damage.

Yaghi, who won the 2025 Nobel prize in chemistry, said the invention would change the world and benefit islands in the Caribbean, which are prone to drought. He added that it could be a solution for countries needing to get water to marooned communities after hurricanes such as Beryl and Melissa, which left thousands without water.

“Hurricanes such as Melissa or Beryl unleashed heavy flooding, destroying homes and crops and impacting thousands of lives in the Caribbean. This devastation is a stark reminder of the urgent need for enhanced water supply resilience in vulnerable areas, particularly small island nations susceptible to extreme weather events,” Yaghi said.

The invention, Yaghi added, could provide a climate-friendly and sustainable alternative to other water sourcing options such as desalination, which may pose a threat to ecosystems when concentrated salty brine is released back into the ocean.

Last month, a UN report said the planet had entered a “global water bankruptcy era” with nearly three-quarters of the world’s population living in countries classified as water-insecure or critically water-insecure.

“Around 2.2 billion people still lack safely managed drinking water, 3.5 billion lack safely managed sanitation and about 4 billion experience severe water scarcity for at least one month a year,” the report said.

In the three-island Caribbean nation of Grenada, which was devastated by Hurricane Beryl in 2024, Yaghi’s invention offers a ray of hope, particularly for Carriacou and Petite Martinique, which bore the brunt of the disaster and face a triple threat of storms, drought and coastal erosion.

“The technology’s ability to function off-grid using only ambient energy is particularly compelling for our context,” said the Carriacou government official and environmentalist Davon Baker.

Carriacou and Petite Martinique, which are still experiencing the after-effects of Beryl, are forced to import water from Grenada to deal with dry seasons, which appear to be getting more intense and longer every year.

“We’re currently considering comprehensive recovery and resilience strategies, and the atmospheric water-harvesting technology Prof Yaghi developed addresses several critical challenges we face: the high cost and carbon intensity, as well as the contamination risk, of water importation; vulnerability of centralised systems to hurricane damage; and the need for decentralised solutions that can operate when traditional infrastructure fails,” Baker said.

Yaghi, who grew up in a refugee community in Jordan, said he was inspired by the hardships he endured in a home with no running water or electricity. Giving his Nobel prize banquet speech, he recalled water arriving at his desert community from the government once every week or two.

“I remember the whisper through our neighbourhood, ‘the water is coming’, and the urgency as I rushed to fill every container I could find before the flow stopped.”

Describing the invention as “a science capable of reimagining matter” he urged leaders to “remove barriers, protect academic freedom” and “welcome global talent”.

“On climate, the hour for collective action has already arrived. The science is here. What we need now is courage – courage scaled to the enormity of the task – so we may gift the next generation not only carbon capture, but a planet worthy of their hopes,” he said.
